reasoning: The base rate of regime collapse in consolidated, long-standing authoritarian states over a single year is very low, typically around 1-3%. The Iranian regime has survived 45 years and successfully weathered numerous large-scale protest movements, major economic sanctions, and external pressure. Although Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei is elderly and regional tensions involving Israel and Iranian proxies are extremely high, the security apparatus (IRGC and Basij) repeatedly demonstrates both the capability and the willingness to forcefully suppress domestic opposition. Furthermore, even if Khamenei were to die in 2025, the resolution criteria specifies that simple leadership succession within the existing system resolves to NO. The opposition remains largely fractured and lacks the organizational capacity to swiftly overthrow the government or the IRGC in a single year. External conflict, short of a full-scale ground invasion by the US (which is highly unlikely), is improbable to directly induce an immediate regime collapse. Thus, the probability of complete regime change in 2025 alone is assessed near the lowest bounded limit.
